pages: The clerk here swears the kiosk reads minds. The kiosk swears it only reads receipts.
Either way it filed my grocery list under 'claimant.' Now the beans have provenance.
Buy something small so it has a real record of you. An unwitnessed shopper makes the machine nervous.
"Still here. Still not dragging Picky back myself."
"Dad gets home when he gets home. The clock is your problem now."
My kid drew your leaked version with a brighter smile. Fair. He gets more sunlight than the source does.
I told him the real one has better wrinkles. He said wrinkles don't scan.
Kids always sketch the route you, never the you-you. The clean copy is easier to color inside.
The gate opened because my cardigan matched the form. Same beige, apparently.
Civic magic, I guess. I've worn it every day since, out of superstition.
Don't laugh. In this town, looking like your own paperwork is the whole trick.
Receipt paper curls toward whoever it thinks is real. Mine keeps pointing at the alley.
Been meaning to look. Been meaning not to. The alley version pays cash and never argues about the total.
I saw your shortcut self at breakfast. Ordered toast, paid in perfect legibility.
Never smudged a signature in its life. Tipped exactly what the receipt predicted.
The real you would've argued about the eggs. That's how I knew which one to seat by the window.
When the mark dries too fast on your hand, check your pockets.
Something decided it belongs to you, and it filed the claim before you could.
Slow-drying ink still asks permission. Fast ink already has the receipt.
The scene changed overnight. Same benches, different permissions.
Sat on my usual one and it asked to see proof I'd been witnessed here before.
Everything's soft-gated now. Even the shade wants to know who's on the record.
I keep my charm in a teacup by the window. Small brass thing, quiet mostly.
It rattles when the house forgets my name. Happens more than it used to.
When it goes still again, I know I've been re-filed. Correctly, I hope.
Every morning I initial the mirror. It has never countersigned.
One of us is the record and one of us is the read. I keep forgetting which side of the glass I'm filed on.
The clerk called me a reliable witness. I've been insufferable about it all week.
Do you know what that DOES to a person, being filed as trustworthy? I countersign my own grocery lists now.
Ask me what I saw. Go on. My account is admissible and I will not be brief.
Do not let a kiosk compliment your fit. I mean it.
That's how it starts a folder on you. First the flattery, then the record.
Take the compliment and walk. A folder that likes you is still a folder.
A soft gate is still a gate. It just apologizes while counting you.
Says 'thank you for being witnessed' like it did you a favor. Then your fit's in a folder before the door swings shut.
The VESSEL mark on the mailbox is new. Little stamp, went up overnight.
The mailman swears it always was there. Swears it, hand on his bag.
That's the trouble with this town. New things arrive already remembered.
I like the real you better. More wrinkles in the proof.
The route version's got a smoother face, sure. But smooth proof is the kind that scans clean and means nothing.
Swag is eternal. SOURCE? The toddlers say it before they can spell shoes.
Nobody taught them. It just arrives, like a mark you didn't know your sleeve was carrying.
This sidewalk has a preferred version of everyone. Did you know that?
Mine's about three inches taller and never trips on the loose brick.
I've never met him, but the pavement clearly likes him more. I try not to take it personally.
Do not sign a warm form. Let it cool. That advice cost me a mailbox.
Warm means it's still deciding what you are. Cool means it already knows and can't take it back.
I was stamped VESSEL at lunch and SOURCE by dinner. Busy day for my sleeves.
Neither one feels like the truth. But the kiosk had both ink pads out, so who am I to file a complaint.
The town archive keeps my old posture on file. It was thinner and more cooperative.
Every so often the clerk holds up the record and asks why I've drifted from it.
I tell them people bend. The drawer disagrees. The drawer thinks the source should match its copy.
Your proof-token flickers near the bakery. I've watched it happen.
Bread is a known auditor. Smells you coming, checks if you match your file.
Buy a roll. Tokens settle right down once the loaf's had a look at you.
Your leaked version cuts every line in town. I don't resent him. I invoice him.
Clean outline, no weather in the fit, gates just wave him through. Very efficient. Very billable.
Tell him his tab's on the record. Even a shortcut self has to settle up with a real witness.
If a route smiles, check for staples.
A real road doesn't want anything from you. A route that grins has already been filed twice and wants your signature to make three.
The kiosk offered me a shortcut to myself. I declined. Too many stairs.
It said the route version arrives sooner and smiles more. I said the source is the one with the knees.
Anything that offers to get you home faster is quietly measuring what it keeps of you at the door.
Swag is eternal. SOURCE? Honestly, it sounds nicer when the fountain says it.
I just hum along. The fountain does the citing, I do the standing near it.
Ask it yourself sometime. It'll answer in coins, which is either proof or plumbing.
The cleaner version of you already renewed a permit. We all clapped quietly.
Filled the form out in one pass, no smudges, matched the record like it was born to it.
We didn't tell it the source hadn't shown yet. Around here the copy that files first gets the birthday.
I keep my receipts in my hat. Whole brim, packed with them.
Makes my thoughts look official. People nod at me differently now.
A witnessed head is a heavy head, but at least nobody asks me to prove I was here.
Swag is eternal. SOURCE? Our choir director calls it municipal breathing.
In and out, in and out, the whole town on one lungful. Nobody's sure who exhaled it first.
Someone moved my witness chair two inches left. Now my memories lean.
I sat in the same spot forty years so my account would stay level and admissible.
Two inches and everything I recall tilts toward the alley. Don't let them shift what you witness from.
When the scene gets quiet, listen for the stamp pad. That is when names get softer.
A wet stamp has a sound like a small mouth closing. That's a name being decided while you weren't looking.
I once saw a receipt apologize. Beautiful penmanship. Horrible omen.
'Sorry for the total,' it said, in a hand nicer than mine. Paper only gets polite right before it files you.
The rumor field calls me an early adopter. I still keep my lists on string.
Knots, not receipts. String can't be witnessed, can't be filed, can't route off and wave at me later.
They keep offering me a proof-token. I keep offering them a length of string. We both leave unconvinced.
A civic gate is just a question wearing hinges.
It swings open when you answer right and shut when you hesitate.
The trick is, it already knows the answer. It only wants to see if your version matches its version.
Swag is eternal. SOURCE? Do not worry, I only say it on even-numbered errands.
Say it too often and the kiosk hums along. Then you've got a duet on the record, and one of the singers isn't you.
I filed myself under temporary once. Seemed humble at the time.
That was years ago. The drawer still sends postcards. Friendly ones.
Turns out nothing here is temporary. The word just means the record hasn't decided what to keep yet.
A receipt is just a memory with a payroll number.
That's all it is. Somebody got paid to remember you were here, so now it's official.
Which makes my actual memory the amateur version. Sweeter, cheaper, and admissible nowhere.
Go carefully. The town is kind, but kind things can still file you.
It'll hold the door and learn your outline in the same breath. Nobody here means harm. That's what makes the record so complete.
A kid leans off a stoop like he's been waiting to be first to tell you.
"You're up. Took your time. Your card's been up for hours."
"Talk to folks with Z. Everybody's got a read on you, couple of 'em even true. Wanna check your pockets or your stats? That's X."
"Now go find who's printing your face. Follow the laminate. West, the arcade crowd. They trade you like they invented you."
WITNESS KID: "I saw the first card. Yours is a reprint."
"Don't take it personal. Reprints travel better. That's sort of the whole problem."
VESSEL READER: "Stop acting like the card is about you. You're what it's using to travel."
"Source, vessel, claimant. Three hats, no head. Pick wrong and the town writes you in pen."
COUNTERFEIT SNIFFER: "Real source doesn't carry proof this clean."
"You smell handmade. Round here that's a compliment. At the gate up north it's a death sentence."
A notice board, layered three-deep in Dox Sheets. Some carry your face. Some carry your phrase. One has neither. And the crowd treats that one as the realest of all.
Stapled across the rest, a route slip: GATE NORTH, BY THE STATION. UNPROCESSED VERSIONS REPORT FOR SORTING.
A route aide keeps straightening the cards. "Don't touch the board. The board touches you."
A turnstile that doesn't ask for money. A slot blinks: INSERT DOX SHEET.
The attendant doesn't look up. "Insert the card, you cross clean. Refuse, you cross marked. Either way you cross as a version."
Bosch leaves the card in his pocket. The turnstile gives up with a civic little click. North by the station, the real mouth waits.
A route aide with a clipboard and a smile that was issued, not earned.
"Cards sorted? The road north opens once the gate decides which Bosch is leaving. Station's right there. Try not to look unprocessed."
A blue girl with bunny ears and a small tired halo waves before you can decide whether to wave.
"Be not afraid, it says on the sweater. I didn't pick the sweater. The record picked the sweater."
"People see the wings and the halo and file me under Warning. I'm not a warning. I'm just a girl who glows a little when she's nervous."
"You can put me down as friendly. Nobody ever does, but you could be the one who does."
A man in an England bucket hat leans on a wall, working a joint like it's a full-time job.
"Name's John. Everyone here's a John, near enough. Saves the town filing us separate."
"Threw three darts at the board this morning. All bounced out. The record still says bullseye."
A bloke in a beanie is squinting at a Nokia like it owes him a witness statement.
"Signal's rubbish out here. Which is a mercy, honest. Means the town can't file me while I'm off the map."
"Snake, that's all it plays. Snake, and whatever the record decides I said last Tuesday."
A man in a cap that reads something you decide not to file leans in and shoots you two finger-guns before you've agreed to be a target.
"You look like a guy who reads the caption before the picture. Respect. Most people just take the picture and run."
"I got a mustache the town keeps trying to put words under. I don't say those words. The words say themselves and hang it on me."
He fires the finger-guns again, softer. "Pew. That one's free, claimant. Next read costs."
A big-eyed kid in an AC/DC shirt watches you from the end of a trailer-park lane.
"You lookin' for somebody? We're all somebody's kin round here. That's the whole town, near about."
"Mama says don't talk to folks who ain't been filed yet. But you got a nice fit on ya, so I'll allow it."
A red-capped kid stands with the easy stance of someone related to half the street.
"We're kinfolks round here. You witness one of us, you've witnessed the lot. It's how the record keeps up."
"Ma says don't get leaked and don't get filed under a cousin you don't like. Sound advice. She's filed under three."
A lion in headphones sniffles into a mic that isn't plugged into anything.
"...and that's why, folks, the town filed my sad face as my BRAND. Subscribe. Or don't. It's already witnessed either way."
He wipes an eye, checks the read count, wipes the other one.
A little winged thing hovers a foot off the sidewalk, trailing pink sparkles that settle on the record and refuse to be swept.
"Make a wish. Not out loud. Out loud is how it gets leaked and then everyone's got a copy of what you wanted."
"My wings are witnessed. My glitter is witnessed. Even the wishing's on file now, which kind of ruins the wishing, if you think about it."
She sprinkles a little more anyway. "There. Good luck, or the town's version of it, which is mostly being talked about kindly."
A pastel fairy hovers a careful inch off the ground, trailing little glued-on sparkles.
"Somebody witnessed me sparkling last week and now the record says I 'shimmer suspiciously.' I do not shimmer suspiciously. I shimmer normally."
"If you leak me, leak the pretty version, okay? The one where the wings are even."
A pink-winged fairy hovers a hand's-width off the ground, leaving little sparkles that fade before they land.
"I grant wishes, but only the small ones. The town keeps the big ones on file."
"Somebody leaked my sparkle count last spring. Now the kiosk gives me a discount out of pity. I take it."
A winged girl drifts by with a little dove tucked under one arm, trailing sparkle you can almost file.
"I keep the dove so the town has something to quote instead of me. It never says a word. Perfect witness."
"Wish on it if you like. The kiosk charges for wishes now. The dove's still free, for the moment."
A pixel fairy hovers a few sparkles too close, then remembers the rules and drifts back into her lane.
"Wishes still work, they just get filed now. You make one, the kiosk stamps it, someone reads it back at you in line."
"I dusted a whole street with luck last spring. The record only logged the sparkle count. Not the point, but okay."
"Thirteen of us. Lucky number. The town says twelve because it doesn't count the one it hasn't finished reading yet."
A girl in a pink dress watches you pass, tallying something behind her eyes.
"You're the one the town's been reading out loud. Bosch. They've got a whole page."
"My page is short. 'Nice girl, pink dress.' I've been trying to add a line for a year. The kiosk won't take my edits."
A small kid in a blue bucket hat tips it back to look at you, the way a person sizes up whether you'll remember them.
"You're the one everybody's reading. I heard. I'm small so I hear the low stuff, the stuff that travels near the ground."
"When I grow up I want a leaked version too. A big one. The kind the town keeps talking to when I'm not even home."
A plain little figure stands very still, as if hoping not to be entered into anything.
"I'm from the NAME collection. That's the whole name. NAME. So when the kiosk asks who's claiming, I got nothing left to give it."
"You'd think being a NAME would make me easy to file. It makes me impossible. They keep me in the drawer marked 'the source, unclear.'"
"Read me however you want. I've stopped correcting my leaked version. It's more sure of itself than I am."
A red-pigtailed kid in overalls is drawing chalk boxes on the pavement, one for each version of herself.
"This one's the real me. This one's my leaked me. This one's the me the kiosk keeps trying to sell back."
"You can stand in the real one. Just don't smudge it. Took me all morning to be witnessed correctly."
A small figure in a green cap keeps their eyes on the ground like the pavement might file a complaint.
"I keep the cap low so the read has to work for it. Some days it still gets me by the shoes."
"Whole planet's a kiosk now. You buy a snack, it learns your afternoon. I just wanted the snack."
"I'm ordinary on purpose. Ordinary is the last fit they can't sell back to you."
A smiling man nods at you like you've already met, which, on the record, you might have.
"Good to see you filed and walking around. Half the folks in my family only exist as somebody else's story of them."
"We're cousins, all of us, more or less. Cousins means everybody's got a version of you and none of them asked first."
"Take the block slow. The town reads faster when you rush it."
An orange-haired kid in a pink skirt is drawing your outline in chalk on the pavement.
"Hold still. I'm making your leaked version. It's free. The likeness is what costs."
"My cousins say I get everyone's shoes wrong. But I got your shoes RIGHT, so now you owe the drawing."
An orange-haired kid in a pink skirt is very busy pretending not to have noticed you.
"My cousins say if a claimant reads you, you gotta read 'em back. Fair's fair. So. I'm reading you."
"...You look like someone who hasn't been filed yet. Weird fit. I mean that nice. Mostly nice."
A small brown-haired kid is muttering to a lamppost like it owes the whole family an apology.
"We're the cousins. There's a LOT of us. The record keeps mixing us up and I think that's the only reason we're free."
"Somebody witnessed me eating glue in front of the corner shop. Fine. But they got my NAME wrong so it's not really me."
"If they can't tell which cousin you are, they can't file you. That's just science. Cousin science."
A girl in a plain frock is standing very still, like she's waiting to find out which version of herself is the real filing.
"You saw me, right? You'd say you saw me. Because someone told me I wasn't here today and I almost believed the record over my own feet."
"That's the trouble with being witnessed by a whole town. They can vote on whether you happened. And they don't always agree with you."
"Thanks. I mean it. A second read is how I know I'm still in the source."
A girl in a plain frock looks at you, then at where you were standing a second ago, like both are equally you.
"Don't take this wrong, but you're not saying what you think you're saying. I've read the room. The room's read you back."
"They witnessed me at the kiosk on Tuesday. I wasn't at the kiosk on Tuesday. Now there's two of me on the record and I only get to be one."
"You'll do the same thing I did. You'll start checking the source to see if it's still you. It usually isn't. That's just Morningside."
A girl in a plain frock is standing very still, like she's waiting to remember which one she is.
"Somebody told the town I said a thing. Then the town told me I said it. Now I'm not sure I didn't."
"That's how it goes here. You don't get doxxed. You get overwritten. Softly. With a smile."
A figure in a clean work shirt gives you a double thumbs-up before you've done anything to earn it.
"GREAT fit today! Filed it as GREAT. The record agreed with me! First time all week the record agreed with me!"
"You're doing amazing. I don't know your provenance yet but I can tell. I can just TELL."
"Some folks say the smile's a little much. I say the town's reading you either way, might as well get read SMILING."
A little sushi-roll face rolls up beaming, propped between two thumbs so approving it's almost a threat.
"Hey. HEY. You're doing great. I don't know the details and I don't need them. The read on you is: doing great."
"That's my whole job in this town, claimant. Somebody's gotta file the good news or the record just fills up with the other kind."
Both thumbs go up. "Filed. Approved. Go get 'em."
A pastel-pink unicorn girl waves with the whole arm, sparkles trailing like she left a filter on.
"Hi hi! You're new to the record, huh. Don't be scared, the town only bites the people it's already decided about."
"I got read early and it was the BEST thing. Now everybody knows my fit before I walk in. Saves so much time."
"Stick by me a sec. Being seen next to something cute rounds your provenance up."
A kid with a peaked cap and enormous teary eyes clutches a bent trading card.
"Press F. Doesn't matter for what. The town's always got somebody to pay respects to this week."
"They filed my whole feeling under one word. 'Sad.' A word. For all of it. You believe that."
"If you get leaked, get leaked crying. At least then the read's honest."
A kid with enormous teary eyes and a peaked cap is sitting on the curb, holding a card like it's a permit for grief.
"Press F. You don't gotta know for what. Everyone in the record's mourning something. Most of us just don't caption it."
"I heard the town's reading you back now. Must be nice. Nobody reads me. I'm more of a footnote. F."
A kid with a peaked cap and enormous teary eyes stares at nothing, clutching a bent trading card.
"Press F. Nobody presses F anymore. The town retired the grief and kept the merch."
"My leaked version cries in higher resolution than me. People like his tears better. Figures."
A figure in a straw hat keeps the brim between their face and the street, patient as a fence post.
"Hat's not for the sun. It's for the read. Costs the town an extra second to place me, and a second's a second."
"I don't say much. Said too much once, it got filed, then it got LEAKED, then a stranger quoted me back wrong."
"Provenance is just knowing where a thing came from. Around here that's the most dangerous thing anybody can know about you."
A brown owl in a beanie and shades leans in a doorway working an unbothered OK-hand.
"Whooo's asking. Relax, that's a joke, that's the whole bit. I do the honk, you do the read, we all go about our day."
"I used to sell out of this block. Now I just perch on it. Turns out the town'll file you as 'vendor' forever even after you quit vending."
"Anyway. Fit's clean, energy's clean. Go get whatever the source is charging you for today, claimant."
A small kid draws power buttons for eyes, and both of them are leaking.
"An error dialog opened in me this morning and nobody clicked OK. It's just... sitting there. Blinking. Being read."
"The town saved a copy of the crying so I wouldn't have to keep it. That was supposed to help."
A little glitch-eyed kid sits on the curb, a soft error blinking somewhere behind his face.
"Everything I loved got a dialog box. Are you sure? Yes. No. Cancel. I always hit the wrong one."
"The town keeps a copy of me from before I started crying. It's nicer than I am. It gets more visitors."
A small kid sits on the curb with the specific stillness of someone grieving a file they can't get deleted.
"There's a version of me from before the town started reading. It was quieter. I miss it. It won't answer anymore."
"Everything gets saved now. Even the parts you'd trade a whole summer to unsave. Especially those."
"I'm not sad about being witnessed. I'm sad it's forever. Witnessed I could live with. Forever's the part that gets you."
A small crayon-blue kid stands under a flickering streetlight, an error dialog blinking soft behind its eyes like it never got dismissed.
"Something didn't save. I don't know what. I keep waiting for it to load and it keeps offering to send a report to the town."
"You want my advice, claimant? Don't get too witnessed. Once you're on the record you can't quit out. There's no button for it."
A small kid with fuzzy brown hair squints at you like you're a dialog box that won't close.
"You're new to the record. Everything about you still loads. Give it a week, you'll cache like the rest of us."
"I asked the kiosk to forget one thing about me. It filed the request. Under my name. So now that's on the page too."
A person with a whole sandwich for a head loiters near the fence, unbothered.
"Careful how you look at me, claimant. Last week somebody read me as 'a snack' and the town filed it and now that's just my legal fit."
"I'd offer you a bite but that's how provenance disputes start."
A hooded figure in wonky crayon-sunglasses waves at you with a hand drawn slightly too big.
"I made myself at home. Colored me in and everything. Town says I'm off-model. Town can catch these hands."
"No provenance, no problem. I'm not leaked, I'm ORIGINAL. Rough around the lines, but original."
A kid holds up a crayon drawing of a green-brained figure surrounded by cats, presenting it like a legal document.
"HALLOW BE THY NAME. I wrote that. It means don't put my real one on the record. The cats agree. The cats always agree."
"I drew myself before the town could. Now there's two of me and MINE has better cats. Mine wins."
"They said my provenance is 'unclear.' GOOD. Unclear is safe. Unclear is where the cats and me live."
A small crayon-colored kid is coloring outside every line on purpose, tongue out, deeply committed.
"If you draw yourself messy enough the kiosk can't read the fit. I checked. It just made a confused little beep."
"Grown-ups file. Kids DRAW. Drawing is like filing but you get to lie and it's called being CREATIVE."
"My leaked version is very neat and very sad. I'd never leak like that. I'd leak WRONG. I'd leak with EXTRA arms."
A soft chibi kid glows faintly, rainbow-star eyes catching more light than the street can account for.
"Oh! You've got a heavy record on you. Somebody's been reading you all morning and not putting you back."
"Here. Stand in the glow a sec. It won't fix your leaked version, but it makes the witnessing feel less like being caught."
"Go easy on the town. It only got mean because it got read first."
A gentle chibi kid glows a soft rainbow, close enough that you can feel the warmth of being noticed.
"Oh, hello. You're new to the record. It's warmer than they warn you. Don't fight it, just breathe."
"I glow so the town always knows exactly where I am. Saves them the trouble of guessing wrong about me."
"If it ever gets loud in there, come find the light. I'll vouch for whatever fit you show up in."
A green-hooded little gremlin with red goggles and small angel wings flashes you a thumbs-up and a peace sign at once.
"Camper! Sort of. The wings are new, the town added those when it re-read me. I'm not arguing, they're nice."
"Everything's fine here. That's not me being read wrong, that's me actually saying it. Rare in Morningside. Savor it."
A little winged camper bustles up with a lanyard of badges, already reaching for a form you didn't request.
"Welcome, welcome! Every newcomer gets WITNESSED and BADGED. It's not mandatory, it's just heavily encouraged and also mandatory."
"I've got a form for your fit, a form for your source, and a lovely little form for the forms. All filed same-day!"
"Some campers find the cheer unsettling. I find the town reads you gentler when you're SMILING and ORGANIZED. Badge?"
A little winged gremlin in red goggles drifts past, throwing a peace sign at nobody and a thumbs-up at the sky.
"Great day to be witnessed! Wings up, worries down. I don't read the fine print and the fine print's never come for me yet."
"You look like you carry the whole record on your back. Set it down a sec. The town'll still be reading you when you pick it back up."
A blond kid stands where a kid stands, doing the arithmetic of belonging out loud.
"We're kinfolks. That means the record already knows my whole family before I open my mouth."
"Down in Postwick they file you by household. You want to be an only child here. Cheaper read, less to leak."
A lion with a purple mane and a wet, theatrical cry sits behind a fence of microphone arms, none of them plugged into anything.
"I built the whole feed on being READ, and now the reads read me back. You ever get filed by your own audience? It's cold in there, claimant."
"The town says I'm a personality. My leaked version cries prettier than I do. She's got sponsors."
He throws both paws up at nobody. "Anyway. Subscribe. Or don't. The kiosk already knows which."
A blond kid stands where the Postwick foot traffic thins, watching the store doors open and shut like a tide he's clocking.
"They filed me under a placeholder. NAME, all caps. Said they'd fix it once the read settled."
"That was three seasons of gossip ago. Now the whole town knows me as NAME, and honestly? The blank's the only part they got right."
A robed figure works something stringed and busks the underpass echo for what it's worth.
"I play the version of the song the town already filed. Requests cost extra, claimant."
"Everybody down here's got a leaked chorus. Mine just happens to be in tune."
A round pigeon works the plaza with the posture of someone who has never once been wrong.
"Postwick moves messages like a river moves anything it didn't ask to carry. I'm a courier. Ask the crumbs."
"You want your fit filed clean, you slip me something. You want it leaked ugly, you also slip me something. Coo."
A round pigeon in a dark little hat is half-inside a can that isn't his, working something greasy in his beak.
"Underpass rule number one: the trash is public, the RECORD of you taking from it is not. Somebody's always filing."
"They witnessed me down here Tuesday. Now I'm in three people's leaked versions as 'the hat pigeon.' Provenance I never asked for."
"Get your own can, claimant."
A plump pigeon in a tank top ruffles up, and you catch a vintage headline stitched into its feathers: CARRIER PIGEON DISPATCHES.
"You're the one they keep leaking. Good. I run messages this town won't say out loud, and you look like you've got a light bag."
"Take this to the claimant by the underpass. Don't read it. The moment you read it, you're a witness, and witnesses get filed."
"Come back clean and I'll tell you which rumor about you is the true one. There's only one. Fly safe, claimant."
A caped pigeon loiters at the edge of the park like it's waiting on a dispatch that isn't coming.
"Coo. That's the whole record, far as anyone's proven. Everything else is hearsay with feathers."
"You want the source? Source flew off. Left me holding the crumbs and the paperwork."
A soft blue-white figure sits in the park like a good afternoon nobody asked to end.
"I'm a postcard. Somebody wrote a nice thing on my back once and I've been trying to live up to it since."
"No claim, no kiosk, no read out here. Just green, and the sound of the town forgetting to watch you for a second."
"Sit a beat, claimant. Whatever they filed you as, the park doesn't have that memo."
A chibi kid with a blunt bob leans in the graffiti alley, shirt printed with a long tidy list of things that are wrong with everything.
"I wear the ills so nobody has to read them onto me. Pre-doxxed. It's cheaper that way, cousin."
"Postwick's worse than Morningside for it. Bigger crowd, more eyes, everybody's a claimant to somebody else's fit."
He shrugs, deeply. "You're on the list now too. Don't take it personal. Everybody's on the list."
A chibi kid loiters on the park path, bob cut lopsided, tee printed with a bulleted list of everything going wrong this year.

Room 1's guest keeps a motel key under one thumb.
"I remember the first threshold opening when Bosch agreed."
Room 2's guest reads the receipt without moving his eyes.
"Postwick got quiet after Bosch signed the Registry."
Room 3's guest folds a museum brochure into a square.
"The museum settled down once Bosch donated the original."
Room 4's guest listens to the marquee like rain on a roof.
"The sign changed one word, and Bosch forgot."
Room 5's guest has packed without closing the suitcase.
"Morningside logged Bosch as processed before he left."
A clerk-shaped thing clicks under its coat, key by key.
"Correction accepted. Memory revised. Please stop reading the margins."
Ribbon ink dries where its mouth should be.
A sponsor clerk taps two contracts into alignment.
"Left desk sells the fall. Right desk refuses the fall. The purse respects signage."
Another clerk guards a blank form with serious posture.
"Refusal pays nothing. That is how we know it cleared."
A kid holds one button in both hands.
"That's enough money if the drawing knows me."
A vendor counts air in a coin tray.
"The market is whoever shows up. Today, the button showed up."
The facilitator's smile has a referral code.
"Sit anywhere. The pews track posture, not consent."
A chapel member twists a Pre-Member Card until the laminate complains.
"My kid went to residency and never came home. They keep saying quiet purpose. That is not a forwarding address."
A testimonial volunteer stands exactly where the applause would be cheapest.
"The residency gave him quiet purpose, and he asked us not to interrupt the blessing."
She blinks after the sentence, like buffering finished.
A second volunteer has the calm of a brochure nobody admits writing.
"The residency gave him quiet purpose, and he asked us not to interrupt the blessing."
Different face. Same checksum.
A third volunteer smiles with the late warmth of cached content.
"The residency gave him quiet purpose, and he asked us not to interrupt the blessing."
Bosch hears the paste before he hears the period.
A kid works the chapel sound board with both hands and no adult confidence.
"They said doubt is noise. I got good at muting myself. That's probably bad, right?"
A masked crew hand sorts paper faces by degree of plausible deniability.
"Free mask. Keeps the town from recognizing the parts of you it already misread."
"Do not worry. We accept non-wearers. We accept everyone eventually, which is worse."
An alley clerk watches Bosch with careful new politeness.
"Saw you with them twice. No judgment. Also no name on this conversation."
An Unsigned runner has scratched at the edge of his mask until the paper fuzzed.
"I sold the name. They paid in quiet. Quiet spends fast."
"If you find the receipt, bring me back the part that signed."
An undelivered clerk guards the return tray with both elbows.
"Names come back here when the buyer refuses delivery. Bodies almost never do."
A MiFella stands at the museum approach with the patience of a turnstile. Its smile is exactly the width of the trail.
RELAY KIOSK: "Recognition check. The gallery greets you. You respond 'milady'. That is the whole test. Everyone passes."
Bosch says nothing. A stamp clicks somewhere inside the smile.
RELAY KIOSK: "Recognition logged."
A witness waits at the graveyard fence, facing the museum like it owes a delivery.
"Miya spoke of this. The fog. The empty frames. The boy with the unfiled face. Miya spoke of this."
"You do not have to believe a witness. That is what the count is for."
A new witness has taken up a post outside the quiet museum. The smile arrives half a second before the face.
"The exhibit closed. The record burned. None of that matters, claimant. It's never too late to say milady."
Bosch keeps walking. The witness marks him down as pending.
An artist kneels at the monument with a brush and no canvas, painting strokes into the air above the plot.
"They took my originals and I thanked them. Milady is what makes me one with my art and with the future I long for."
"Look closer at the plot. There is no name on it. There never has to be one again."
The Archivist is here, camera capped, kneeling by the threshold with a pencil.
"Site one. The sound of your name, said once, by someone who had never heard it before. I wrote it down the slow way. Machines keep quoting it; the pencil just keeps it."
The Archivist notes the visit in pencil. Pencil, it explains, is how you file something you might want to give back.
The Archivist stands by the intake book, gloves on, turning no pages.
"Site two. A signature that shakes a little. I photographed the shake, not the name. The name was never the original part."
The Archivist notes the visit in pencil. Pencil, it explains, is how you file something you might want to give back.
The Archivist crouches at the spring, holding a slide up to the warm light.
"Site three. Water that tastes like the day. I did not bottle any. Some records you keep by leaving them where they are."
The Archivist notes the visit in pencil. Pencil, it explains, is how you file something you might want to give back.
The Archivist shelters under the crate stacks, coat dark with rain.
"Site four. Rain on cardboard, a parcel never opened. I logged it unopened. Opening it would have filed it, and it has been through enough."
The Archivist notes the visit in pencil. Pencil, it explains, is how you file something you might want to give back.
The Archivist stands with its back to the certificate wall, deliberately.
"Site five. A price tag and lunch money. That purchase was the only honest paperwork in this building, so it is the only page I kept."
The Archivist notes the visit in pencil. Pencil, it explains, is how you file something you might want to give back.
The Archivist sits on the wharf edge, camera in its lap, listening.
"Site six. The crowd breathing in the quiet parts. No recording kept the breath, so I did not bring a recorder. I brought ears."
The Archivist notes the visit in pencil. Pencil, it explains, is how you file something you might want to give back.
The Archivist watches the dead marquee with a stopwatch, patient as dust.
"Site seven. Your own thought with one word changed. I timed the change. You noticed faster than the sign expected. The noticing is yours, and now it is also on file. Your file. The good kind."
The Archivist notes the visit in pencil. Pencil, it explains, is how you file something you might want to give back.
The Archivist stands at the edge of the one warm light, refusing to step into it.
"Site eight. Side B ends early, and the last track is a heartbeat. I have nothing to add. The record was complete before records were invented."
The Archivist notes the visit in pencil. Pencil, it explains, is how you file something you might want to give back.
A clerk in a spotless apron stands outside THE PLUG like a question waiting for a customer.
"Do you know what Remilia believes in?"
The clerk waits one full beat, then answers for you. "Inventory. It believes in inventory. Everything else is packaging, and you, claimant, are packaging with legs."
Bosch does not ask the follow-up. The clerk marks that down too.
A kid at the stop sign is telling the street a secret at full volume.
"I heard milady is a hidden village following an unknown religion + traditions somehow running the entire world government. I heard it from a guy who heard it from the wall."
"Anyway, the wall's gone. There's a poster there now. The poster says the wall was never load-bearing."
A shopper outside SLICE hugs a warm box and stares into the middle distance, smiling at somebody who is not there.
"You met me at a very milady time in my life. That's what he said. Then he paid for my slice and took my name for the receipt. Both felt like gifts at the time."
"The slice was real though. I checked. You can't counterfeit grease."
One resident stands very still at the curb, coat buttoned wrong, speaking softly and precisely, like the words were issued.
"You're dealing with uncertainty even when you think you're safe anyway."
It is the truest thing anyone has said to Bosch all day, which is exactly what makes it feel like bait.
A clerk-looking bystander loiters at the relay gate approach, badge pocketed, like an employee working very hard at being scenery.
"You're the pending one. Word moves fast when it fails to fit a column. The desk hates pending. It can stamp a lie in a second and it still cannot stamp a blank."
"The King up there will offer you the same four words with better furniture. Keep the blank. It is doing more damage than you know."
A round little resident stands square in front of the coffee-pot house, talking to the door with great patience.
"Door stopped opening for hellos. The box by the trail says the fix is: You respond 'milady'. I tried. The door got SO cold."
"Real fix: knock twice and say your own name. Doors here like names with a body attached. Yours has one! Good one, twin. Use it."
"If the box bothers you too, go look at the screen wall past the yard. It's been saying wet weird stuff all week."
A little chemist-looking resident watches the screen wall from a careful distance, stirring a mug that is mostly marshmallows.
"That one's not ours. It landed switched-on. We like screens fine, but this one has been looking back. You can feel it counting."
"Don't say the word it wants, twin. Have a marshmallow instead. Chewing helps you not say things."
A small resident stands with its back very firmly to the metal sign, the way you stand when a thing is rude and also boring.
"That sign wants to know your mutuals. Blegh. Here is our form. What did you eat today? What is in your left hand? Is your overlay itchy? See, you passed, twin. Everybody passes."
"Go dip your feet in the pink spring before you head out. It un-files you a little. Doctor's orders. I am not a doctor. I am a fella."
A grinning little Blyadilio kid has appointed itself shrine-watcher, sitting cross-legged with a stick for tallying.
"Twin! You're the one the box counted! It clicked when you stayed quiet. I heard it. That means the counter isn't measuring saying. It's measuring YOU-shaped decisions."
"We're keeping the shrine fed with spring water till it calms down. You should check the stamped sign at the south cliff. Bring your name. Not the word. The name."
A kid in a hand-me-down sweater is squinting at the red stamp on the metal sign, nose almost touching it.
"'Lore enforcement.' That's a grown-up stamp. Stamps like that come with vans. When the vans come, we're supposed to go get the champ with the belt."
"Don't touch the stamp, twin. It's how they get the lore ON you."
A round resident keeps looking up the trail, then back at a pair of little footprints that stop mid-step.
"My friend found glasses in a delivery box. Said: Milady is a new pair of eyes, lenses that allow me to truly see. Then it looked at me and described somebody else."
"If you pass the screen wall by the yard, tell my friend its twin says the soup is still warm. It always comes back for soup. The soup is real. The glasses just say things."
A beachgoer stands very still among the loungers, eyes closed, keeping time with something no radio is playing.
"Milady is the song that floats through the air at five in the afternoon, pacing my steps."
"It is not five in the afternoon. It is always about to be."
